comments.title: John Carter on the Podcast Was Never Found and Question About Vibrating Words Outside of Magic
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To all who mark it, a blessed Samhuinn - may the close of this year's life bring bounty and harvest, and the opening of the next seeds ready for growth. For anyone else, happy Halloween!

To Share: For those who do podcasts, Mark Bisone's The Podcast Was Never Found recently hosted his friend John Carter, and their discussion was wide-ranging and interesting. Maybe most interesting to those here was some fairly extensive speculation on the role of narrative in metaphysics and/or how we choose to relate to what happens to us and the wider world - it's certainly at least an interesting way to look at what it is we're doing here in incarnation: https://markbisone.substack.com/p/tpwnf-episode-2-the-dark-jeddak-rises

To Ask: I had a question on the "Heathen Rosary" I posted last week, and it had enough of a magical side to it that I thought it would be worth asking here. In brief, it's a set of prayers with a set of beads modeled on the Catholic Rosary, but with prayers to Heathen Gods and Goddesses and with some different numerlogical symbolism. After each prayer, you say "Alu" where a Christian would say "Amen." The reader asked if it would be worthwhile to vibrate ALU in this sequence. I wasn't sure, as Alu is certainly a word of power that seems worthy to vibrate in the right place, but the sequence of prayers isn't a magical ritual, which is the only context for which I know of vibrating words of power. So, would this be helpful, harmful, neutral, or something to determine by experiment?

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: John Carter on the Podcast Was Never Found and Question About Vibrating Words Outside of Magic
replies.content: I'd encourage practitioners to experiment with that. Vibration, for those readers who don't know Golden Dawn jargon, is a way of chanting that sets up buzzing or tingling sensations in or around the body of the practitioner. All things considered, it's not that different from Gregorian chant, and even less different from the way that mantras are chanted in many south or east Asian traditions. It might be suitable for your rosary, or it might not -- more likely still, it'll be suitable for some people but not for others. Experimentation is called for.

replies.title: Re: John Carter on the Podcast Was Never Found and Question About Vibrating Words Outside of Magic
replies.content:
Thanks very much! It seemed like that would be the likely answer, but I wanted to make sure there weren't any known pitfalls before haring off after giving it a try.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: John Carter on the Podcast Was Never Found and Question About Vibrating Words Outside of Magic
replies.content:
A few related observations:

I recently came across music called Norse Throat Singing that sounds like vibration to me.

Second, Ted Nottingham, who writes and produces videos on esoteric Christianity and the Fourth Way, recently posted a video where he said that if prayers are offered on a rosary for an extended period of time, say a full day, or perhaps more, that even after the prayers stopped, the rosary beads themselves can continue to emanate the prayer out into the universe.

replies.title: Re: John Carter on the Podcast Was Never Found and Question About Vibrating Words Outside of Magic
replies.content: Nottingham is quite correct. Even less intensive work with a rosary, or any other object used to focus and direct prayer, will charge the object with the intention of the person who prays, so that it continues to radiate that intention. If you ever have the chance to handle reverently a rosary that's been owned and used by one of those old ladies who prays with it every day, pay attention to what you perceive -- it can be pretty intense.

replies.title: Re: John Carter on the Podcast Was Never Found and Question About Vibrating Words Outside of Magic
replies.content:
Thank you for both of these! I have heard of throat singing (though more as something that the Eskimo do, didn't realize there was a Norse tradition), but hadn't connected it with vibration, and I hadn't thought of the physical beads a "resonator" of the intentions prayed with them, but that's quite powerful.

Thanks again!
Jeff

1) Do you have any starting points or recommendations to read up on what was called in the 1970's biofeedback?

2) What do you think of it? Does it work?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's been a very long time since I last heard anybody discuss biofeedback! Real biofeedback works, and works well enough that the medical and pharmaceutical industries made a huge effort to bury it -- quite a few illnesses, including things like high blood pressure, were treated effectively by it, and that didn't sit well with the "a patient cured is a customer lost" crowd. There's a certain amount of quackery in the biofeedback field, to be sure, but less than there is in mainstream corporate medicine.

I'd suggest avoiding anything published after 1980 or so, as the 1970s were the golden age of biofeedback. Barbara Brown's Stress and the Art of Biofeedback might be worth your while.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG
I am spending difficult days with a close relative admitted to the hospital, in a serious condition. For this reason I have been thinking about some questions:
1 - When we came to this plane, did we have a pre-established time of life? I know that this time can vary downwards, but how can I vary it upwards and upwards?
2- what kind of cosmic entities establish our time on this planet?
3- If there is a need or even many requests (prayers), can this time be increased?
4 - What is the relationship between suffering and our spiritual growth? I think this topic has already been discussed here…
Thank you very much!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Most of these things vary from soul to soul and from incarnation to incarnation depending on the fine details of karma and destiny. Some souls come into incarnation with a strict limit on how long they will stay, others have more or less wiggle room. It really depends on what goals the soul has (that's destiny) and what burdens it carries from previous lives (that's karma).

The higher self, the aspect of each soul that exists outside of incarnation, plays the most important part in determining the length of possible life, but it's a complex matter in which many entities, discarnate and otherwise, play a part. It's always worth praying, but please remember to pray for the highest good of all concerned, and remember also that sometimes the answer you get will be "no." The cosmos is a complex place and your preferences may not be the best option.

As far as suffering and spiritual growth, there are two sides to that. First, there's karma; if you inflict suffering in one life, you will experience it in another. Second, suffering is necessary to shake us awake to higher levels of awareness. The only way to learn is to fail, and the only way to realize that we've failed is to suffer for it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you for this.

I'm in a very similar position; my uncle is sick with liver failure and it appears he is not much longer for this world. Your advice in paragraph two re prayer is spot-on for me.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not the OP

"Second, suffering is necessary to shake us awake to higher levels of awareness. The only way to learn is to fail, and the only way to realize that we've failed is to suffer for it."

To what extend can this be overcome by conscious effort? I meditated a lot on the life of family members and their suffering, for example. What I can say that at some point a certain realization was reached which also opened up an emotional component as well as a few events of highly relevant synchronicity. At that point I am unable to say whether my efforts linked my own - at the moment consciously unremembered - memories with the fate of my relatives or if there was genuine empathy at work - probably both. But judging from the experience I dare to hypothesize: If an individual has reached a certain amount of actual experience it might be possibly to generalize this to a certain extent and learn from corresponding emotions stirred up in meditation instead of learning by "big drama"? Which would, if correct, imply that said individual would need to reach a minimum level of development on the mental plain as it would require rather abstract principles (the generalization of the emotions of others) to create and guide astral experience (the emotions the individual experiences during meditation)?

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There's something I can say -- it very likely won't help your present situation, but you did say "upwards and upwards", and not just "upwards", so it sounds like you're asking a question about the structure that would need to be encountered if you did find a way to push the limits.

I would expect that the Unseen are always juggling a bunch of potential plans, adjusting their choices of threads of plan to be materially realized according to various competing priorities and various newly-coming-into-focus opportunities and blockers. However, I don't know when questions of lifespan and next-life arrangements might shake out "well in advance" during these adjustments, requiring a lot of upheaval and creating a lot of poorly-retied loose ends to switch over to a next-best option late in the game, or when they might instead be just as up in the air as everything else.

With respect to the priorities of the Unseen (which have some relation to the priorities of the soul), a single lifespan is subject both to what economists call "increasing returns" effects and to what they call "decreasing returns" effects. During a lifespan, the soul acquires an increasing amount of context and expertise for how it works to be that particular kind of person. So, later in that life, it can practice and learn skills and habits for being functional and effective as that kind of person, and the habits will be more refined, and more compounded on top of other habits, than the habits that would be available to be learned by a soul who hadn't lived that life as long. Likewise, the consequences of one's habits might become more interpretable, since one would have become more of a consistent kind of thing whose habits had more consistent kinds of consequences. Those would be major sources of increasing-returns effects. At the same time, after those habits and interpretations had progressed beyond a certain point of refinement, further improvements might be less valuable or meaningful outside the context of that one life, relative to what could be learned by switching over to another life. That irrelevance would be a major source of decreasing-returns effects. The Unseen's general target for how long a life should be would have a lot to do with the question of when the increasing-returns effects became slow enough that they were cancelled out by the decreasing-returns effects.

Also, one of the Unseen's other priorities would have to do with the problem of maintaining an overall tendency towards consistency of material cause and effect. (This is related to what Dion Fortune called the "initiation of the nadir".) If medical technology were more advanced, people might live longer, at least in some sense. (I read a lot of speculation in this and the other forum about how the senile-dementia epidemic reflects a deficit between the state of medical technology and how long it can keep people alive, versus how long it generally remains beneficial to souls to stay tethered to material consciousness. I have read elsewhere that a lot of our efforts to reduce age-related dementia, especially Alzheimer's disease, have been disrupted by remarkable amounts of scientific fraud. Presumably of course it wouldn't have cost too much for the Unseen to nudge people more susceptible to scientific fraud towards getting into that part of gerontology, relative to the future savings that would result from an epidemic of dementia causing souls to be more untethered from still-living bodies later on. For what it's worth, though, a lot of dementia seems to be a result of chronic systemic inflammation, similar to a more extended form of "chemo brain" or neurological insult from immune response to an intense virus infection.)

I am not sure what the relation of the tales of immortal (and mostly Eastern) alchemists to this hypothesis would be. Maybe, since they have come closer to meeting the Unseen's priorities halfway than materialist medicine has, the Unseen is motivated to come closer to meeting their desires for immortality halfway. More concretely, I wouldn't be too surprised if the crucial factor was that the alchemical procedures in question work partly as a microcosm of what the Unseen hopes to achieve with multiple lives. The vices that are cleared away by death and the virtues that are (at least speculatively) regenerated by reincarnation are, as I understand it, in Taoist alchemy loosely associated with the transition from Metal to Water. Metal corresponds to old age, planning, control, Scottian "legibility", rigid assumptions, and strategizing around remaining strength, and Water corresponds to... I guess dealing with (usually obscure and "illegible") elements of your character that would ultimately have to kill you if they weren't dealt with -- usually, by trialing elements of character to start over with, until you find some that you would have better reason to trust. (Perhaps Yin Water for dealing with things whose presence would kill you, and Yang Water for dealing with things whose absence would kill you.) That's sort of like what people call "formlessness" I guess? I don't know much about Taoist immortality concepts but I understand that quicksilver is often mentioned in that connection, which isn't so surprising since it looks like a way for something that's still a metal to possess certain attributes of water.

Correspondingly, to make it more systemically feasible and sustainable for someone's lifespan to extend "upwards and upwards", it might be necessary to fold in more and more of the ultimately-necessary functions of death into their ordinary ongoing life processes, until it wasn't clear where death ended and life began. (Well. I mean, fold in more of those functions, anyway. Stillbirth in response to before-conception genetic damage, and cellular apoptosis in response to within-lifetime genetic damage, are already such functions. And the immune response to cancer is a harder such function, for if apoptosis fails. I'm not sure what it means that people pursuing mystical contemplation are reputedly more susceptible to cancer; but maybe it is an expression of how they're in contact with a slightly more indiscriminate form of life, and so fail to have proportionately quite as much of the right aspects of death. And all of that is biological, when other necessary forms of renewal would be psychological and sociological and probably a few more -ologicals we haven't even invented yet.) Doing that right seems like a civilizational project on probably a greater scale than the whole history of, say, Taoist alchemy, and probably even greater than the whole history of Western medicine.

And if that were sufficiently taken care of, improvements in medical science and technology would perhaps also be necessary. (If they weren't already intrinsically bound up in the project. The same general principles ought to apply to figuring out what to do when some of your habits incorporate hard-to-replace dysfunctional assumptions and when some of your cells incorporate dysfunctional hard-to-replace genes. And, conversely, the reason our senile dementia research has failed seems to have a lot to do with failure of our scientific funding processes' "immune response" to researchers with "cancerously" fraudulent grant-acquisition strategies. Like, https://twitter.com/MicrobiomDigest/status/1848101502734504352 points to a rundown of Alzheimer's investigators tied to falsified data none of whom have had their labs taken away.)

I say this as someone with strong transhumanist leanings. An excellent materialist exposition of one of the core principles involved in the subject matter of your question can be found at https://gwern.net/Backstop .

comments.title: Intolerance
comments.content:
Morning John,
I’m not sure if this fits in with the usual questions but anyway I’ll give it a go. I’m in my 50s now, and I’ve discovered I’m becoming intolerant to certain foods (rather than allergic). I struggle to digest sugar (that’s the worst), onions, and spicy food. It results in pain, gas and occasionally evacuation. It can’t get away with more than a gram of sugar in any food which rights off most processed foods. I’m aware your late wife had difficulties with food. Medical advice to date is simply to avoid the relevant foods. I’m wondering if there is anything magic/spiritual I can do? Should I try homeopathy? I’m concerned it will get worse and worse restricting my diet to very little choice. I appreciate you can’t give medical advice.
Kind regards Averagejoe

replies.title: Re: Intolerance
replies.content: I would encourage you to consider talking to a naturopath, a practitioner of traditional Chinese medicine, or an Ayurvedic physician. That kind of intolerance can be symptomatic of some broader imbalance that can be treated.

replies.title: Re: Intolerance
replies.content:
You have my sympathies. Just to relate a similar story, about 4 years ago I became aware I was getting these mysterious headaches that felt like pressure in my forehead (not an ache like a traditional headache). I couldn't think straight while I had them. It took me years of groping about and guessing to eventually figure out that I had a glutamate intolerance. Rather inconvenient. The only thing so-far that does much to alleviate my stress levels (which aggravate it) and headaches was following Aryuvedic advice for people with my dominant dosha (element, basically), which is vata (wind, air, ether).
I find it very funny that when it comes to food tolerance problems that aren't strictly allergy (which involve a complex web of interrelated systems) allopathy, which almost always misses the forest for the trees when it comes to human health, the only thing it can recommend is "lol just don't eat stuff."

comments.title: something perplexing
comments.content:
Dear JMG

I have been struck hard by the questions from the anonymous correspondent on the past 2 MMs regarding parasitic family patterns of energy / psychic vampirism. What they recount is similar to experiences of my own, even down to the dreams.

Like that family, there are redheads throughout mine. Unlike them, it is as a group very strongly Catholic down to and including my parents' generation, while I, cousins and siblings were all educated in religious schools. Not that the older individuals would have countenanced what they were doing consciously, so far as I can tell, being quite sincerely religious. Younger ones may have got into manipulative behaviours through inborn aptitude perhaps sharpened by willpower literature such as Dale Carnegie or the Secret type of New Age books.

How much do you believe that kind of self-development movement might foster such dark sides of the personality? Obviously the malign psychic influence can happen regardless of reading or instruction.
And what do you think is happening in terms of its occurring in spite of (even contradiction of) the avowed beliefs of such a natural witch?

replies.title: Re: something perplexing
replies.content:
That's actually fairly common. People who recognize, however vaguely, the presence of those potentials in themselves quite often flee to a religious tradition to try to ward off the nastiness. You're quite correct that the more selfish end of New Thought is a very risky thing for such people to engage in, since their abilities combined with a self-centered attitude very often result in astral vampirism and similarly ugly things.

The difficulty is that you can't prevent those abilities from turning nasty by running from them to conventional religion. You have to master them and use them for strictly positive purposes. Such a person can learn to bless instead of cursing, heal instead of harming, and so on -- but it has to be done deliberately, by taking conscious control of the inner capacities and putting them to work in accordance with will guided by strict morality.

comments.content:
I've picked up running over the last few months as a means to get into better physical shape. I've noticed it seems to have a decent amount of possible overlap with occultism. First, there's a lot of willpower required to do it, especially with longer or faster runs. There's also self-knowledge: most runners eventually learn certain foods cause problems if eaten shortly before a run, for example; while the most common way to get an injury from running is running too far, too fast, or too much, so a knowledge of how much you can run is essential.

Then there are the energy centres. Running gets the heart beating, and extended over longer periods of time, it seems to cause sensations similar to the ones I get when I do the Middle Pillar Ritual, or another that works heavily with that centre. It takes far longer, but my sister, who runs marathons, but has zero occult practice, was able to confirm this occurs even in runners who have not done anything to activate the heart centre. The side stitch is also a very interesting phenomena in light of body energy centres: runners who push to hard will often get a pain at the solar plexus; this can be avoided by learning to breath properly, which given the role of the solar plexus and breathing in bringing etheric energy in seems like a fascinating connection. This also means that runners often have to work on breath control, which is another overlap with occultism.

In A Magical Education, you said that there are certain "auxiliary arts" (forgive me if that's the wrong term, I do not have access to the book right now) that mesh well with occultism, and if I remember correctly provided martial arts as an example. Do you know if anyone has explored running as a possible auxiliary art for occultists?

If you choose to pursue running in those terms, though, by all means go for it -- it ought to work very well.

"I haven't heard of anyone else taking up running as an auxiliary art to magic; weightlifting is quite another matter"

Could you expand on the weightlifting angle here?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There was a lot of overlap in the very early 20th century between occultism, the natural-living movement, and what was then called "physical culture" and we now call exercise. On the one hand, a lot of occultists recognized that if their material bodies were as strong and healthy as possible, that gave them many advantages in occult work; on the other hand, a lot of people who were into natural living and exercise realized that basic occult methods could be used to improve their capacity to overcome obstacles and achieve their dreams.
[IMAGE:0]
This is Joe Greenstein, a Polish-American Jew who became one of the major figures on the strongman circuit under the stage name The Mighty Atom; he used Cabalistic incantations and meditations to amplify his considerable strength. This is a guy who could, and repeatedly did, tie a #2 iron horseshoe into an overhand knot with his bare hands.
[IMAGE:1]
This is Genevieve Stebbins, the Jane Fonda of women's exercise at the beginning of the 20th century. She was also a very high-ranking adept in the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor, one of the leading occult orders of the time.

These are the two figures on the interface between occultism and exercise that I know most about. There were thousands of others. Most of them weren't famous; they were ordinary men and women who decided that being strong and healthy beat the bejesus out of the alternative, and used the power of their minds as well as their bodies to get there. A lot of them lifted weights -- free weights, please note, so they could develop the little stabilizer muscles all through their bodies. They liked kettlebells, dumbbells, and Indian clubs:
[IMAGE:2]
It was good robust stuff and I hope to see it revived someday.

comments.title: Conceptualization vs Imagination
comments.content:
Hello JMG and thank you again for being there for us!
Kinda a silly question but would the word “conceptualization” work as another way to describe how “imagination” works for the mage? In other words, are those terms interchangeable?
Best
Jill C

replies.title: Re: Conceptualization vs Imagination
replies.content: I see them as related but not interchangeable. To conceptualize something is to express it as a single clear concept, and that requires you to understand it thoroughly on an intellectual level. To imagine something is to express it in a single clear mental image, and that requires you to grasp it clearly on a nonverbal level. Consider a leaf. To conceptualize a leaf is to understand what it is, what it does, and why it's shaped and structured the way it is; to imagine it is to build up a mental image of it that, to you, expresses "leaf-ness" as clearly as possible.

comments.title: Raising kids
comments.content:
What's the best way to raise kids in an occult household? I'm specifically wondering the following:

1. What age to introduce them to basic banishing rituals / meditation / SOP?
2. What objects to place in the home?
3. How to protect babies / young children from negative spiritual forces?

replies.title: Re: Raising kids
replies.content:
Well, keep in mind that I haven't raised kids, so my ideas will be very theoretical! Children can be introduced to natural magic (magical herbs, protective amulets, healing teas, and so on) from infancy on. Meditation's best left until 7 or so, when they have enough control over impulses that they can start learning to concentrate, and basic banishing rituals are best left until after puberty is well under way -- with boys, wait for the voice to start breaking; with girls, wait for the breasts to start developing.

As for your second and third questions, natural magic is all but universal in human cultures to bless and protect children from before birth all the way through childhood. The details will depend on what kind of natural magic you work with -- European herbal magic has one set of practices, traditional Southern conjure has a different set, and so on -- but whatever method you choose, it's a good way to keep everyone safe and happy. If you're comfortable with some form of religion, of course, invoking divine powers through prayer and the like is also well worth doing.

replies.title: Re: Raising kids
replies.content:
I'm raising multiple curious kids and navigating these issues on a regular basis. No prescriptive advice, just what is working for us right now:

1. My two youngest are very interested in my daily rituals, though I normally do them before they wake. When I do them later, even in a secluded spot, one of them often finds me and wants to be near - similar to our cats who also seem drawn by the Sphere of Protection. I generally discourage my kids from hanging around my ritual work, though one in particular is very drawn to it, and is also quite energetically sensitive. He has asked to be taught the SOP but is still too young. I've opted for teaching him prayer, and visualizing light, essentially teaching some basic Modern Order of Essenes style clearing. All my kids are over 7 and they still struggle with sitting meditation, though we still try. I find that labyrinth walking works better, and better still is observational meditation. We have successfully worked with the nature observation practices from the Druidry handbook (like picking a square foot of ground and observing multiple times.)

2. Myself and other occult friends have recently been lamenting that we erred too far on the side of not indoctrinating our kids into our beliefs, discussing our beliefs and practices too little where it would have been better to discuss them as often as they were pertinent and on our own minds. I think this plays into the objects in the home. I have been working this past year on owning my spaces and pulling my sacred objects out of hiding. I increasingly believe in fully living my own beliefs, and offering my children the regular reminder that they are free to question, challenge, explore, and believe differently.

3. Multiple times, I was able to soothe a screaming baby in the backseat by visualizing them bathed in light, warmth, and love. I have also had success relieving nightmares with prayers to ancestors, guardian angels (whom I work with even without being a Christian,) and in extreme cases doing banishings in the bedrooms of the afflicted. We've also had success with dreamcatchers. For my family the spiritual protection works similar to the physical protection - we try to stay clean and balanced as parents and pay attention, when the kids talk or have fears, we listen and take them seriously.

Wishing you well!

replies.title: Re: Raising kids
replies.content: You may find the 20th century occultist Karel Weinfurter's book Mysticism for All: Especially for Parents of interest. I wrote a short review here.

replies.title: Re: Raising kids

replies.title: Re: Raising kids

While my magical and occult experience is limited, I do have quite a bit more experience with practices of the subtle anatomy (qigong and yoga), and some deity propitiation. I recommend the simple and gentle introductions to qigong and yoga for children ages 3-14 (above that's outside my expertise), simply because they really are just getting used to being in a physical body still. For meditation, I recommend learning to concentrate on physical objects for short periods (3-5 minutes), like candlelight or other natural phenomena. I don't recommend mindfulness. You can also safely make amulets for them from safe, protective gods like Athena, Minerva, and Venus (the decidedly Roman, not-syncretized-with-Aphrodite versions). Sorry to say, but your other questions are best left to JMG and others as I can't answer them. Hope that helps!

replies.title: Re: Notion of God/Ultimate Source in Qabalah
replies.content: The Cabala is, if anything, even more unwilling to say anything about the Absolute than Hindu philosophy. The ten names of God assigned to the spheres of the Tree of Life are forms or manifestations of the divine, comparable to the gods and goddesses of the Hindu faith. Above them are the three veils of negative existence:  Ain Soph Aur, which means "light without limit;" above that, Ain Soph, which means "without limit;" and above that, Ain, which means "Not." Above that, finally, is the Divine in its own essential nature. So if we look beyond the manifested forms of the Divine, we see limitless light; if we look beyond that, we see infinite space; if we look beyond that, we see nothing at all -- and beyond that, where we can't look at all, is the Divine.

comments.title: What's in a blessing?
comments.content:
Good Morning JMG and Commentariat,
I was reading through last week's magic monday and saw your comment about domestic pets being animals much closer to their reincarnation in human form. This sent me into a lovely day dream where, in the divine planes of existence, all the deities we humans primarily interact with are the obsessive-pet-owners and crazy-cat-ladies who choose to hang out with and care for us and post their stupid-human and cute-human pics while the other divine beings just roll their eyes. Odin, Frigg, and Thor as quirky homesteaders who went off grid with their funny farm of norse peoples. The Olympians as posh uptown apartment dwellers with litter boxes everywhere and going for walks with their pets in strollers... That was fun imagery!

1. I may have thought myself into a corner and am hopeful that you, JMG, or the commentariat, can see something obvious to help lead me back out.

For a while now, I have been quietly offering blessing to others using MOE methods and blessing water and items using the technique from the gnostic lessons. I initially offered a simple "may you be blessed." The wording I use has been evolving along with the process as I don't work with a single clear pantheon; I figured out that some of my waffling and stalling was tied to over-rumination on deities and pantheons and related mythology reading. I paused all that to get out my head and more into my practice. Now my name for the divine moves through various forms of "the Divine," "The One Life," "The Powers of Nature," "The Eternal Spiritual Sun," "The Flow of the One Life," etc. My current best understanding of a relatable supreme divinity is "the powers of nature flowing the one life," conceptualized as one life polarized into two flows moving through the elements and all beings. Somehow in this process, what I'm intending with blessing has become... complex. (I'm open to the possibility that the conception of divinity and the complexity are connected but not causal.)

The complexity that often now arises when I offer blessing: When I think of blessing as "good" intent, who am I to know another's good? If it is the good known to the divine rather than my conception, then what is the purpose of injecting myself in the process? I've tried just intending that the currents of life flow through the subject of my blessing or intending balance; though strangely that feels like a greater imposition - I seek flow and balance, but how am I to know if that is what's needed by the subject? I was offering blessing in moments where I was frustrated or otherwise hard-hearted toward someone or something. Initially, that practice helped me see my biases and helped open my heart; now when I attempt it, I sometimes almost feel like wishing wellness, balance, or flow could even be a curse to some people for whom that process would be unsettling at best and world-shattering at worst. So, I'm stuck between blessings that feel meaningless and one's with meaning but feel improperly imposing.

I feel like much of this is a breaking down of hubris around my personal spiritual journey and an increase in the awareness of the selfishness inherent in practicing altruism while embodied; yet, I want to continue to offer blessing.

Any suggestion or direction, or personal stories of overcoming such an impasse would be appreciated.

2. In a similar vein, I've thought about how to better work with personal intentions and asking for blessings upon myself. Once again, it can feel toothless to ask for a simple blessing - I feel gratitude for the flow of life through me and that is a clear blessing. I worry that asking for things specifically can lead to the vending machine form of religion I'm avoiding. Yet I also am working to become balanced, conscious, and active in the flows of life, and there are times that specific help would be, well, helpful. I've been experimenting with the thought that asking a specific deity or power of nature for blessing with the right headspace could be more like aligning myself and attuning my perception so that I can better witness and interpret the blessings that are flowing around at all times. I'm working most consistently with the WGS and it seems like the affirmation work in there and the modifications to the SOP post initiation somewhat work in this way. I also suppose that the clearest way to avoid vending machine relationship is cultivating real, two-way relationship with the beings and powers I work with - though when there is a power imbalance, the two-way-ness seems somewhat suspect.

Do you (or anyone) have any thoughts on this? How do you conceptualize the ethical asking for and receiving of help from non-corporeal entities?

Thank you as always for this space and your counsel.

replies.title: Re: What's in a blessing?
replies.content:
1) This is why, when I can't get formal consent, I prefer phrasing such as "I offer you a blessing." If the person doesn't want the energy you offer them, that affirms their right to turn it down. You can't know what another being needs; you can at best say, "I offer you this energy with my positive intention," and let them take or leave it as they choose.

2) This is why it's helpful to work with deities and spirits whose interests and intentions you already know. If you invoke the oyarsa of Venus for help learning to love, say, you're going to get a favorable response because that's what she wants and likes and constantly tries to do -- you're saying to her, "Hi, I know this is your gig, if you'd like to work through me I'd be grateful." If you want to be more balanced, conscious, and active, choose those entities that rejoice and delight in those things and work with them, offering yourself as a vehicle for which they can accomplish their work.

From the start, I've offered blessings with the intent that they can be turned down, "may you be blessed if you so choose) being the primary form it takes in my mind though the words often simplify to may you be blessed. My hangup at this point is that my mind gets hung up on even that simple phrasing - what is it that I even mean by blessing? How do I form a positive intention if I don't perceive or fully understand a being or situation? I may also have slipped into that space where you say and ponder something so many times that it loses meaning. Like writing a word the hundredth time and forgetting how it's spelled.

As I type this, I'm thinking about your post on Schopenhauer, and wondering if perhaps a way out of the quandry is to view blessing as just a moment of aligning my will with what I feel is powerful and beneficial and then focusing toward another in hopes that they benefit...

2) That makes sense, practically and helpfully. Thanks again.

replies.title: Re: What's in a blessing?
replies.content:
Hi Hearthculture,

Some thoughts from personal experience - take whatever is helpful to you and discard the rest! :-)

1. Do you need to have a name for the divine in order to bless? Or could this, maybe, just be a way to make things so complex and convoluted that you'll stop doing the work? ;-)

> When I think of blessing as "good" intent, who am I to know another's good?

In my experience, you don't need to know what is good for them. The Divine will know this much better than you, so why interfere? Simply offer a blessing, and leave the rest to somebody else.

> If it is the good known to the divine rather than my conception, then what is the purpose of injecting myself in the process?

This is just a model, but maybe it helps: In my understanding, when I bless others, I'm a combination of conduit and pointer - I point the divine blessing energies to where they might be welcome or desirable, and I help bring them into the material world by allowing them to flow through me.

Could the divine do this in a different way? Sure, but this way seems to work, and who am I to question this?

(Besides, all the blessing energy is flowing through me, too - and to me this is a major point in the MOE work.)


2. Does it have to be either-or? I.e. could you ask for general blessings for yourself on some occasions, and for specific ones on other occasions? Also, there is a wide range of "specific" - you could e.g. ask for "health" in general, or for some very specific health outcome through a certain treatment, or for various things in between. Maybe there is a sweet spot for you somewhere in this range?

In general, my impression is that it's ok to ask for something - although asking doesn't necessarily mean you'll get it. You're simply pointing something out to the Divine.

As to the vending machine question: Well, do you only ever ask for blessings for yourself? Or do you mainly bring blessings on other beings, and then also to you as an aside? :-) The Divine isn't a vending machine either way, but I feel there is a difference between only ever wanting things for yourself vs. wanting good things for everybody (or, well, almost everybody - we're all just human!), and thus also for yourself.


Hope this helps! Btw, your pet-owner daydream is hilarious! :D

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: What's in a blessing?
replies.content:

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: What's in a blessing?
replies.content:
Howdy,

Just to add another perspective, again, to take or leave as works for you:

1) Similar to what milkyway1 said, I try to see myself as more of a conduit than an actor, and to leave as much of what "good" looks like to beings smarter and farther-sighted than me. That being said, and tied a bit to 2) below, I do sometimes try to think of and offer (not force, of course) more specific blessings, which I mostly see as an exercise in empathy for me. Simple example: as many do, I get frustrated often when driving. I've made it my default to try to catch myself and offer the person frustrating me blessings (sometimes with the caveat "nonetheless" if I'm still kind of sour, or even "May you be blessed, even if I can't offer it" for when I'm really salty). Sometimes, though, I try to remind myself that these folks, like me, are just trying to get where they're going, and likely making the best decisions they can with where they are. Whatever good the blessings may or may not be doing for them, it seems like making the effort is helping me better attune to the kind of energies I want to be in touch with.

2) I tend to look at asking for specific things in prayer or as blessings as a) helping me get more honest about what I want (which helps make it easier to adjust that where it needs it), b) acknowledging that I need help and that like every other man, I am no island, and c) better aligning parts of myself and my life with the sources of particular blessings.

So, to give a rather poignant example for a), before sleep, I pray to Thunor and Frigg in thanks for watching over my children and ask them to please keep doing so, but, like with almost all of my other prayers, I add "nonetheless, your wills, not mine, be done." Actually confronting what "putting things in the Gods' hands" means as starkly as "even if it means not protecting my daughters" is, well, really hard. But it also clarifies how important that is to me, which helps me to better do whatever I can on my end.

For b), I'm pretty fond of my own agency, so I think it's good for me to focus on the fact that everything I have and do relies on things others have given or left me. Asking the Gods for help is a good way for me to bring that in to my day-to-day life. Any help actually given is an even better reminder that I didn't do that "all by myself," and I try to take it in that vein.

Lastly, for me, c) means that praying for specific things from specific Gods requires thinking about "which God should I ask? how should I ask for it?" which helps me come to understand that God better. For one personal example, despite Woden being the "All-Father," I don't pray to Him for help with "being a better father," for that I tend to pray to Thunor, which is a habit I picked up through trial and error praying to both, including for some specific things.

Anyhow, as I said, not sure if all of this will be helpful for you, but hopefully some of it is, at least as a spur to thought.

Cheers, and, appropriately enough, my blessings if you'll have them, and in whatever form you and the divine find best for you :)
Jeff

replies.title: Re: blessings walk

There's no necessary connection between what you can do and what you can feel. It's quite common, in fact, for a student's ability to affect things to pick up much more quickly than their ability to perceive things; I've quite often had students tell me that it doesn't feel as though they're doing anything, when their Essene healing or some other kind of work was intense enough to rock me back on my heels.

If you want to develop your sensitivity, meditation is crucial. The Essene healing breath meditation is especially good for that, but any form of meditation will help.

As for your encounters, yep -- this stuff really does work. Especially these days, when there's so much negativity in the world, when somebody goes by radiating the positive energy of blessings, a lot of people notice, and some will stop and stare.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,


1. What is the difference between praying to your Higher Self versus your HGA?


2. Is one of these easier to communicate with? 

a. If so, which one?

b. Are some methods of communication better than others for each one?


3. Can one of these beings/states of consciousness do things the other can't?


Thanks as always for Magic Monday.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, let's start with some basics. Your higher self is the real you -- the self that you were before you were born and will be after you die, the you that gathers up the memories and lessons of every incarnation. My teacher John Gilbert used to say that we're like people who put on gorilla costumes and then ended up thinking we're gorillas; your personality, the person you think you are, is the gorilla suit, while your higher self is the person inside the suit.

Your guardian angel (the Jewish and Christian term) or guardian genius (the Pagan term) is the spirit who has been guiding you since the beginning of your incarnations. It can't make you do anything -- all it can do is advise you. Its voice is one of the things that gets called "intuition" and also one of the things that gets called "conscience." The more you listen to it, the clearer its voice becomes.

Neither of them is a spirit you can summon. Your higher self is you, so there isn't much point in praying to it. Your guardian angel or genius is always present, and if you simply make a point of speaking to it and then listening to it -- right after your daily meditation is a good time for this -- the channel of communication will get wider. This is, in my experience, a much better way to do the thing than the kind of ornate ritual you get in the Abramelin book and equivalents.

comments.title: The Reiki Wrecking Ball
comments.content:
Years ago, I got a Reiki Grade I initiation. Of course, life happened, and I completely forgot about it, and never really used it (and it "helped" that I didn't feel a thing happening to me at said initiation, which of course means nothing, but when you're doubting anyway...). Pretty exactly one year ago, though, the urge suddenly rose up in me to practice Reiki again - maybe because I had started doing the SOP, and then one day, the Reiki folder we received in the course fell into my hands again.

Anyway, I did one (1) self-treatment. I don't even remember if I got through all the hand positions. I had no intention other than to start practicing again, so I did this in the evening, after work, and went to bed.

Over the course of the next weeks, my car broke down, then I had to wait three weeks before it got repaired, and then I totaled it on the first day I could drive it again. The next week, someone broke into my house; the week after that, I got covid. A month after that, my landlord told me that he needed the house (which I had been living in for ten years at that point) for his son, and that I needed to move out ASAP. To say that I found myself in a whirlwind of panicked activity is an understatement. I must add that my old car was a black hole for money, constantly needing repairs, and that the landlord was an alcoholic who neglected his property and never reacted when I called him about repairs, and the house as a result was moldy and slowly decaying. I very much wanted to move, but I felt stuck.

Well, one year later, I have a new (small, used, but well-kept) car, a wonderful new home, and a substantial pay raise (which I had to ask for in order to afford the move). I'm in a much better state now than I was before, but that year was *intense* - I worked hard to get to where I am now, and most of the time, I was panicking LOL.

When I told my sister all of the above (I am blessed with a sister I can talk 'woo-woo stuff' with!), she commented that this Reiki session must've knocked loose some blockages and sent me on my way to a better future (or present). Intuitively, I agree with her, but as I said, getting to where I am now was a frightening experience.

The problem is, I feel the urge to practice Reiki again, but I don't want to swing another wrecking ball at my life. I haven't even unpacked all my boxes yet!

So, err, is there a way to sort of direct or soften that energy? Or will it just zap me where I'm weakest? I plan to resume my SOP et al practices as soon as I've settled in, but I'm not sure if this will help, because I was doing the SOP last year, too.

comments.title: Nothing Happens by Accident
comments.content:
Happy Monday, JMG,
As if on queue, your book cover this week segues into my only question very nicely. Dreams have been intense the last two days. Last night was vivid and comic book like in quality: an alien space craft that descended for a closer look at our existence, churches of immense scale, record stores I could (and probably have) spent a lifetime in. Giants that looked suspiciously like Norse gods walking above and past the motel where I hide with my family. Trump flags flew through the sky, some attached to aircraft, others up there on their own. And the night before, I saw a creature I can honestly say I had never seen before in dreams, a very animated octopus. Black with intense red eyes, he seemed to be running from something but once we crossed paths, he stopped. Using those eight arms, it seemed like he was trying to tell me something that I could just not comprehend. About the only thing I came away with was that something large was near. Then I woke up. My wife has a book of dream interpretation but much to my chagrin, it had no listing for an octopus. My question to you and anyone else who may know a thing or two about such matters, what does an octopus in dreams signify?

replies.title: Re: Nothing Happens by Accident
replies.content: Everything I've learned about dream interpretation says that there is no one thing that any dream image means. The meaning of that octopus will be a matter of what your own subconscious, and nobody else's, means by it. With that in mind, you might try some journaling. Write "What does the octopus in my dream mean?" at the top of the page, then blank your mind and write down whatever surfaces, no matter how odd or absurd it seems. Don't edit or censor anything. See if that gives you some clues.

replies.title: Re: Nothing Happens by Accident
replies.content:
I'm not sure oneiromancy really makes sense as a form of divination that people can usefully pass around "codebooks" for, other than perhaps some basic archetypes. I think of divination practices as being like languages. Like in Tarot, there's some standard interpretation conventions, but then most advanced practitioners develop their own private interpretation conventions on top of those, and the unseen divinatory-judgment-generating process seems to take account of those conventions. Oneiromancy seems like it would be much more on the extreme end of "private interpretation conventions".

You might have a look at the current slate of pending apocalypses currently being bandied about in the near-mainstream. Without putting my thumb on the scales too much, I have an opinion as to how there's one putative apocalypse in particular that withstands scrutiny better than the other proposed apocalypses having the same proposed degrees of scope, severity, and probability. This apocalypse does have some symbolic connections to octopuses, but they are extremely circumstantial or relatively tangential. The connections would go through the observations that (1) octopuses have eight legs, and (2) if you made a scatterplot of "how smart this animal is" versus "how dissimilar to humans this animal is", octopuses would be one of the data points making up the outer edge of the cloud of points. (Although there is one other eight-legged animal further along the outer edge of the cloud, the Portia genus of spiders.)

But maybe there would be such circumstantial or tangential connections with any plausible "large" thing that would be "near". Maybe, once you've done the scrying-like exercise with the blank paper, it would be a good exercise to try enumerating candidate possible large near things (a category which, I grudgingly admit, also contains non-apocalypses), and, for each proposed thing, counting how many connections you could draw between it and very animated intense-red-eyed fleeing black octopuses trying to nonverbally convey incomprehensible concepts.

comments.title: Arboreal Meteorologist?
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. Here’s a strange one for you. In early of March this year I was ambling around in the bush by the creek that is very close to my house (it is a nature preserve / park filled with beaver and deer and all sorts of wild critters!) and I was drawn to a row of old maples planted by some farmer at least 150 years ago. One tree in particular attracted my attention, so I went up to it and leaned against it with my back for a good while. The tree seemed pleased. I then invited the tree to talk to me if it felt so inclined. The reply that I got was unexpected: it said, “expect heavy rains in April and June”. That was all. And until today I promptly forgot about it. Reflecting upon the maple tree’s message, I do recall that April was a wet month (I had to empty out my back yard a few times with a siphon) and then in the first week of July we received the heaviest rainfall event in 19 years (complete with highway closures and associated pandemonium which, fortunately, I was able to avoid). Not a bad prediction – especially since the July flood happened on the new moon day that brought to a close the moon of June (I assume that trees are more familiar with the lunar calendar than our silly Gregorian calendar).

So, my question is: have you either heard of, or experienced a tree predicting weather?

Mint Raunchy Owl aka Ron M

replies.title: Re: Arboreal Meteorologist?

Nature spirits and nonhuman beings of various kinds were always consulted by the old druids and shamans, because they're tapped into the network of nature to a much greater degree than we are. Growing up, I always heard that you should watch the squirrels in the fall, because the more frantic they are about harvesting and stashing nuts, the harder the winter will be. Old strong trees will know a lot from personal experience as well.

comments.title: Number of Human Incarnations, Interval/Time Between Incarnations
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

What is your understanding/belief about the approximate number of times each individual soul or Monad reincarnates within the human kingdom, and the average length of interval between earth lives? I've entered esoterica through theosophy and anthroposophy, and I recall Blavatsky stating we spend on average the equivalent of 1,500 earth years between incarnations. Yet I believe Rudolf Steiner says we each incarnate only seven times in each kingdom; and that our afterlife time spent specifically in the astral realm or kama loka tends to amount to one-third of our earth life...i.e. the amount of time spent in sleep/dream state. Many channels and authors also posit that we incarnate much more frequently, e.g. in the subsequent generation, with our parents now as siblings or offspring etc. I understand this would vary greatly from soul to soul and path to path, but on balance/average what are your own estimations?

Many Thanks for your wisdom!

replies.title: Re: Number of Human Incarnations, Interval/Time Between Incarnations
replies.content: It really varies. Just now a lot of souls are having lives close together because there's such a surplus of human bodies to fill; back before the global population got so high, things were less rushed, and as global population declines -- it's beginning to slide right now -- things should calm down again. Some people also have lives close together because they don't have a lot of karma and can use a fairly common kind of life, others have lives far apart because they have a lot of karma to process between lives and they need a life of a kind that doesn't come up often. Nor is there a fixed number, or even a meaningful average number, of lives at the human level. it all depends on whether you screw up (nearly all of us do) and how badly you respond to the consequences (this varies drastically).
